mnemonics: Phony Hares Usually Test (P-HUT): P-Value, H₀ (Null Hypothesis), α (Significance Level), Test Statistic
Type I Error: False Positive (Falsely Rejecting H₀). Type II Error: False Negative (Failing to Reject a False H₀)
keyPoints: A hypothesis test evaluates two mutually exclusive statements (Null H₀ and Alternative H₁) about a population using sample data.
The p-value is the probability of obtaining results as extreme as the observed data, assuming the null hypothesis is true. A small p-value (≤ α) provides evidence against H₀.
The conclusion is always about the null hypothesis: either 'reject H₀' or 'fail to reject H₀'. One never 'accepts' H₀ or 'proves' H₁.
commonMistakes: Confusing 'failing to reject H₀' with 'accepting H₀'. A lack of evidence against H₀ is not proof that H₀ is true.
Misinterpreting the p-value as the probability that the null hypothesis is true. It is the probability of the data *given* that H₀ is true.
Using a two-tailed test when the research question specifically predicts a direction (e.g., 'increases' or 'decreases'), which requires a one-tailed test.
